subject: Chemistry
solution:
When Ni2+ is replaced by Pt2+ in the complex NiCl2Br22-, the following properties are expected to be changed:

A. Geometry: Since Pt2+ and Ni2+ have different coordination preferences and atomic sizes, the replacement is likely to result in a change in the geometry of the complex. That is it changes to square planar.

B. Geometrical isomerism: If the replacement of Ni2+ with Pt2+ leads to a change in the ligand arrangement, it can result in the generation of cis and trans isomers.

C. Magnetic properties: PtCl2Br22- is diamagnetic.

Both the complex species are optically inactive.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Ferric ion forms a prussian blue precipitate due to the formation of Fe4FeCN63 . In this method, to a portion of sodium fusion extract, freshly prepared ferrous sulphate FeSO4 solution is added and warmed. Then about 2 to 3 drops of FeCl3 solution are added and acidified with conc. HCl . The appearance of a prussian blue colour indicates the presence of nitrogen.

FeSO4+2NaOH→FeOH2+Na2SO4

6NaCN+Fe ( OH ) 2 → N a 4 [ Fe ( CN ) 6 ] Sodium ferrocyanide +2NaOH

3N a 4 [ Fe ( CN ) 6 ]+4FeC l 3 → F e 4 [ Fe ( CN ) 6 ] 3 Ferric ferrocyanide ( prussian blue ) +12NaCl

subject: Chemistry
solution:
For $[Pt(NH_3)(H_2O)BrCl]$ with square planar geometry and four different ligands, geometrical isomerism arises from different positional arrangements: X = 3.
For $[CrCl_2(ox)_2]^{3-}$, the trans isomer is optically inactive (meso compound) while cis is optically active, giving Y = 1.
For $[Co(NH_3)_3(NO_2)_3]$ in octahedral geometry, facial (fac) and meridional (mer) isomers are possible: Z = 2.
Therefore, X + Y + Z = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6

subject: Chemistry
solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is square planar, diamagnetic $(0$ unpaired electrons) with $d s p^2$ hybridisation. 
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ - is tetrahedral,diamagnetic ( $0$ unpaired electrons) with $s p^3$ hybridisation. 
$\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ is tetrahedral, paramagnetic ($2$ unpaired electrons) with $s p^3$ hybridisation. 
Hence, the option (c) is the correct answer.

solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mi>CoCl</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>absorbs with higher wavelength of light with respect to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> because <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Cl</mi><mo>-</mo></msup></math> is weaker ligand than <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. The crystal field stabilisation is higher for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>Co</mi><msub><mfenced><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></mfenced><mn>5</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math>, hence we can say that wavelength of light absorbed depends upon the oxidation state of the metal ion.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
The complex $\text{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O}$ with magnetic moment 3.87 BM indicates $3.87 = \sqrt{n(n+2)}$ giving n = 3 unpaired electrons, confirming Cr³⁺.
A 1:2 electrolyte means structure is $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O})_5\text{Cl}]^{2+}\text{Cl}_2^-$.
Molar mass = 52 + 5(18) + 35.5 + 6(18) = 285.5 g/mol.
Moles of complex = 2.75/285.5 = 0.00963 mol.
After cation exchange, 2 Cl⁻ remain per formula unit (from the 1:2 electrolyte), giving 0.00963 × 2 = 0.01926 mol of free Cl⁻.
Reacting with excess AgNO₃: mass of AgCl = 0.01926 × 143.5 = 2.76 g ≈ 3 g (nearest integer).

subject: Chemistry
solution:
d-orbital is degenerate which split into two levels eg and t2g in the presence of ligands. This splitting is due to the presence of ligands. This is called the crystal-field splitting and the energy difference between the two levels is called the crystal-field splitting energy, Δ0.

(A)  Ti2+:4s03d2

t2g e–=2  eg e–=0 

Δ0=2-.4Δ0=-.8Δ0

(B)  V2+:4s03d3

t2g e–=3  eg e–=0 

Δ0=3-.4Δ0

= -1.2Δ0

(C)  Mn3+=4s03d4

t2g e–=3eg e–=1 

Δ0=3-.4Δ0+1.6Δ0

=-1.2Δ0+0.6Δ0

= -0.6Δ0

(D)  Fe3+=4 s03d5

t2g e–=3  eg e–=2 

Δ0=3-.4Δ0+2.6Δ0

=-1.2Δ0+1.2Δ0=0

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Ni(CN)42-:Ni+2=

It is diamagnetic as  CN- is a strong field ligand and forces electron pairing.

Ni(CO)4:Ni=

It is diamagnetic as carbonyl group is a strong field ligand and forces electron pairing.

NiCl42-:Ni2+= 

It is paramagnetic as chloride group is a weak field ligand.

Fe(CN)64-:Fe2+ :- 

It is diamagnetic as –CN is a  strong field ligand which causes pairing of electrons.

CuNH34+2:Cu+2⇒ Here Copper ion has one unpaired electron, thus it is paramagnetic as it is 3d9 configuration.

Fe(CN)63-  :  Fe+3: 

It is paramagnetic, here CN- is a strong field ligand

FeH2O62+:Fe2+:

It is paramagnetic, here H2O , here a weak field ligand

solution:
In octahedral complex \((\mathrm{CN}=6)\)
If \(\Delta_0 \lt \) P.E., then high spin complexes are formed
If \(\Delta_0 \gt \) P.E., then low spin complexes are formed But in tetrahedral complex \((\mathrm{CN}=4)\)
\(\Delta_t \lt \) P.E., then mainly high spin complexes are formed and rarely low spin complexes are formed.
